feedback.text: Greetings. I would like request the possibility of adding Videogames to the Digital Fairness Act on the basis of recent unfair practices committed by videogame companies. The videogame industry itself has become very lucrative and very unregulated when it comes to consumer rights. For example, Ubisoft, one of the biggest videogame companies on the market, has many concerning aspects when it comes to consumer fairness. Within their EULA, they state that they can terminate a user's license agreement at any point for no given reason. This is a word by word located within their EULA "You must immediately uninstall the Product and destroy all copies of the Product in Your possession". This also legally binds all users to destroy physical copies of Ubisoft games, which is an outrageous thing to demand in general. Additional wording and extensions to their License Agreement leaves them with enough legal space to avoid lawful issues, however the customer has no way to seek recompense for any injustice done towards them. "The Crew", a racing car videogame from Ubisoft, which was previously available on Ubisoft's digital shop was discontinued by them. However, they then proceeded to permanently delete the game itself from unsuspecting users accounts, which they had paid full price for in the past. While this is a unjust situation covered by their anti-consumer EULA, the customers were left without a product they paid for. While the Stop Destroying Videogames initiative nearly has 1.5 millions signatures, we understand that some of the current games cannot be made to last forever due to the foundations they were built from at the beginning of early stage development. However, I believe it is not unreasonable to have fairer customer practices enforced on all games that are made after the initiative is further discussed, developed and accepted. While we do not want every game to be kept alive and supported forever, we would like to requested the availability for a post-discontinuation plan by developers for all future games so that customers can still retain their purchased product without fearing that they will lose access to it forever or that it will be taken away from them. While no support for discontinued games is expected, being able to keep the installation files so that we can work towards getting the game up and running on compatible systems so that we can ones again enjoy our purchase is not unreasonable, I believe. As it stands, the current unregulated gaming companies are providing a service more akin to renting a videogame, rather than buying one. Since buying implies obtaining ownership of the software, renting fits more with what they have been doing in regards to removing access from customer accounts. This is even more egregious as there is no indication on how long this renting service will last for until ownership of the purchased content has been lost or will be taken away. Due to this, if a game has no post-discontinuation plan and the company has no plans to make it playable upon sunsetting it, I believe it to be fair to the consumer to label the "Buy" button as "Rent". With a statement confirming that the game has no end of life plans from the company. All relevant material has been attached to the provided .docx file. While it is not in as much detail as I want them to be, I respect your time and just added general and specific information about my points. While all of this is just a fractions of the injustices done towards the everyday videogame consumer, I hope I have managed to at least share some knowledge of the occurring and potential malicious practices which are happening in this huge industry. As people who are passionate about games, please allow us to defend our rights to own the purchases that we make and to be able to enjoy them without the fear of them ever being taken away unjustly. Thank you very much for this opportunity to allow me to express myself and for the Digital Fairness Act.
